stem: What does a single point in MRI k-space represent?

options.text: The brightness of one corresponding image pixel
A spatial-frequency contribution containing information from the entire image
The T1 value of one corresponding voxel
A physical location occupied by one receiver coil

explanation: K-space is a spatial-frequency domain, so every point contributes globally to the reconstructed image. A two-dimensional Fourier transform combines the complete dataset into image pixels.

stem: Which image properties are most strongly determined by the center of k-space?

options.text: Fine edge detail only
Slice position and RF center frequency only
Overall signal, contrast, and broad low-frequency structure
Chemical identity without image brightness

explanation: Central k-space contains high-amplitude, low-spatial-frequency information that drives contrast and gross anatomy. It also contributes strongly to image signal-to-noise ratio.

stem: Which image property is most strongly associated with peripheral k-space?

options.text: Overall contrast between large uniform tissues
Longitudinal recovery before excitation
Absolute main magnetic field strength
Fine detail and sharp edges

explanation: Peripheral k-space stores high-spatial-frequency information, which defines rapid changes and edges. Removing peripheral data produces a smoother or blurrier image despite retaining broad contrast.

stem: In a conventional two-dimensional acquisition, what typically selects successive k-space rows?

options.text: Different phase-encoding gradient amplitudes
Different patient table positions during one echo
Different RF coil diameters
Different T1 values within the slice

explanation: Readout samples traverse one k-space line while the phase-encoding amplitude sets that line's position in the orthogonal direction. Repetition with new phase steps fills the matrix.

stem: Motion during acquisition of central k-space is especially likely to alter which image feature?

options.text: Only the highest-resolution edge pixels
Image contrast and broad ghost structure
Only slice thickness
Only the displayed field-strength label

explanation: Central k-space contains much of the image's energy and contrast information, so corruption there has conspicuous global effects. Peripheral corruption more selectively degrades detail and sharpness.

stem: What is a principal tradeoff when fewer than all planned k-space lines are acquired to shorten a scan?

options.text: T1 and T2 relaxation stop occurring
The main magnet no longer needs shimming
Reduced data can decrease SNR or create reconstruction-related loss of detail and artifact
Every omitted line is replaced by an identical measured line

explanation: Partial acquisition exploits k-space symmetry or reconstruction assumptions to save time. Because less independent data are measured, signal, resolution, or artifact performance may suffer.
